title: Network key support in `.pod` file
body:
Working on converting the quadlet in the Forgejo install docs to a pod that includes MySQL, I ran into a problem where I couldn't access the exposed ports from the pod using the `.network` they define in the example. If I removed the network entirely and let quadlet figure things out, it worked fine.

https://forgejo.org/docs/latest/admin/installation-docker/#podman

I saw the following discussion on the original issue for pod support and tried passing the network via PodmanArgs in the `.pod` file after creating the network in their `.network` file as mentioned, and that works. 

So I'm following Valentin's suggestion to open a new issue to ask for the `Network` field to be added to the `.pod` file :) 

> @ygalblum Thank you so much for adding this feature so quickly. I tested this out last night and it works great. I just have one question, as there does not appear to be support for `Network` at the moment, I'm wondering what the recommended approach would be for this.

Perhaps just manually create a network and assign it via `PodmanArgs` ?

```
[Pod]
PodName=mypod
PodmanArgs=-p 8000:80 --network=my-manually-created-network (can't set a .network file this way though it seems.)
```

_Originally posted by @drewlsvern in https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions/17687#discussioncomment-7712583_

Host details:
podman --version
podman version 5.2.2

comments.body:
Thanks for pointing that out, we missed that. Looks like we were doing something supported without knowing it.

It looks like since we were converting from an existing simple quadlet to a pod and *reusing* that network definition, the network got removed at some point. The oneshot nature of the generated `-network.service` meant the network wasn't recreated on a simple service restart. 

A reboot likely would have created the missing network correctly, but manually stopping the _active(exited)_ `-network.service` before restarting the pod also worked for troubleshooting this today.
